faqs.question: How did people travel in the old days without cars?
faqs.answer: In the old days, people mainly walked, rode horses, or used bullock carts. For longer distances, they used horse carriages or trains (after railways were built). Boats and ferries were used to cross rivers. Travelling to another city could take days or even weeks! Today, we can reach the same places in just a few hours by car or plane.

faqs.question: How did people communicate before phones were invented?
faqs.answer: Before phones, people wrote letters on paper and sent them by post. Letters could take days to weeks to reach. For urgent messages, they sent telegrams - short messages sent through electric wires. People also communicated by sending messengers on horseback. Town criers would walk through streets announcing important news loudly.

faqs.question: What games did children play in our grandparents' time?
faqs.answer: Children played many fun outdoor games like gilli-danda (with a stick and small piece of wood), kho-kho, kabaddi, marbles (kanche), hopscotch (stapu/langdi), and pitthu (seven stones). They also played with tops (lattu), flew kites, and played hide and seek. They made their own toys from clay, cloth, and sticks. Most games needed no equipment at all!

faqs.question: What is a chulha and how is it different from a gas stove?
faqs.answer: A chulha is a traditional Indian stove made of mud or clay. It burns firewood or cow dung cakes for fuel. Cooking on a chulha takes longer and produces a lot of smoke. A gas stove uses LPG gas, cooks faster, is cleaner, and can be controlled easily. Many villages still use chulhas, but gas stoves are becoming more common through the Ujjwala scheme.

faqs.question: How were schools different in the old days?
faqs.answer: In the old days, many children could not go to school, especially girls. Students wrote on slate boards with chalk instead of notebooks. There were no computers, projectors, or smart boards. Schools had strict teachers who sometimes used a stick. Books were shared and very precious. Today, education is a right for all children, and schools have modern facilities.

faqs.question: How did people keep food fresh before refrigerators?
faqs.answer: People were very clever about storing food! They dried fruits and vegetables in the sun, pickled them with salt, oil, and spices, and made jams and preserves. Grains were stored in big clay or metal containers. Milk was boiled and converted to curd or buttermilk quickly. Earthen pots (matkas) kept water cool naturally. Some people stored food in cool underground rooms.

faqs.question: What has NOT changed over the generations?
faqs.answer: While technology and daily life have changed a lot, some important things remain the same: the love between family members, the joy of celebrating festivals together, respect for elders, helping neighbours in need, storytelling by grandparents, and the importance of hard work and honesty. Values and traditions are the things that connect us to our past.

faqs.question: What is a generation?
faqs.answer: A generation is a group of people born around the same time who grow up together. You and your classmates are one generation. Your parents are the previous generation. Your grandparents are the generation before that. Each generation is roughly 25-30 years apart. Comparing generations helps us see how life has changed over time.

faqs.question: Why is it important to learn about how life was in the past?
faqs.answer: Learning about the past helps us appreciate the comforts we have today, understand the hard work of earlier generations, and learn from their wisdom. It teaches us that change is natural and constant. It also helps us preserve our traditions and culture. Plus, the stories of our grandparents are fascinating - they are like a living history class!

faqs.question: How can I record my grandparents' stories?
faqs.answer: There are many fun ways! You can write down their stories in a special notebook, record them speaking using a phone, draw pictures of the things they describe, or make a family scrapbook with old photos and written memories. You could even make a video interview. Ask them about their school, games, food, festivals, and daily life. These memories are precious treasures!
